days.title: I have, you have: имам in the present tense

vocab.exampleEn: I do not have a phone.

grammar.title: The verb имам (to have)
grammar.explanation: Bulgarian uses the verb **имам** to say what you have. It is fully regular: аз **имам**, ти **имаш**, той/тя/то **има**, ние **имаме**, вие **имате**, те **имат**. The negative is built with **нямам** (a fused form of не + имам): аз **нямам**, ти **нямаш**, той **няма**, ние **нямаме**, вие **нямате**, те **нямат**. There are **no noun cases**, so the object after имам keeps its plain dictionary form: имам чанта, имам телефон. To ask a yes/no question, add the particle **ли** after the verb: Имаш **ли** ключ?

examples.english: He does not have a key.

reading.title: Имаш ли ключ?
reading.text: — Имаш ли ключ? — Не, нямам. Но имам нов телефон. — А пари? — Днес нямам пари.
reading.translation: — Do you have a key? — No, I don't. But I have a new phone. — And money? — Today I have no money.

days.speakingPrompt: Say three things you have and one thing you do not have right now. Use имам and нямам with everyday items (телефон, ключ, чанта, пари).

days.title: My and your things: the short pronouns ми and ти

grammar.title: Short possessives ми (my) and ти (your)
grammar.explanation: The easiest way to show possession is with **short possessive pronouns** placed right **after the noun**, and the noun usually takes its **definite article suffix**. For 'my' use **ми**, for 'your' (singular, informal) use **ти**: чадър**ът** **ми** (my umbrella), чант**ата** **ми** (my bag), очил**ата** **ти** (your glasses). These little words never change form. Remember the article is a suffix: masculine **-ът/-а**, feminine **-та**, neuter **-то**, plural **-те**. Note that **ти** here means 'your', while **ти** as a separate subject means 'you' — context makes it clear.

reading.title: Нещата ми
reading.text: Чадърът ми е тук. Часовникът ми е стар, но е хубав. — А портфейлът ти? — Портфейлът ми е празен.
reading.translation: My umbrella is here. My watch is old, but it is nice. — And your wallet? — My wallet is empty.

days.speakingPrompt: Point at four of your belongings and name each with the noun + definite article + ми (чадърът ми, чантата ми, очилата ми). Then ask a friend about one of theirs using ти.

days.title: His and her things: the short pronouns му and ѝ

grammar.title: Short possessives му (his/its) and ѝ (her)
grammar.explanation: We continue with the third-person short possessives. For 'his' or 'its' use **му**; for 'her' use **ѝ** (written with a grave accent to tell it apart from the conjunction и 'and'). They go after the noun, which carries its article: куфар**ът** **му** (his suitcase), раниц**ата** **ѝ** (her backpack). The pronoun does **not** change for the gender of the thing owned — only for the owner. So 'his bag' and 'his suitcase' both use **му**: чант**ата** **му**, куфар**ът** **му**.

reading.title: Куфарът и раницата
reading.text: Куфарът му е голям и тежък. Паспортът му е тук. Раницата ѝ е малка, но снимката ѝ е хубава.
reading.translation: His suitcase is big and heavy. His passport is here. Her backpack is small, but her photo is nice.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe one item belonging to a man and one belonging to a woman. Use му and ѝ with the noun + article (куфарът му, раницата ѝ) and add an adjective like голям, малък or тежък.

days.title: Our and your(plural) and their things: ни, ви, им

grammar.title: Short possessives ни, ви, им (our, your-plural, their)
grammar.explanation: The plural short possessives finish the set: **ни** (our), **ви** (your, plural or polite), **им** (their). As before, they follow the noun with its article: кол**ата** **ни** (our car), билет**ите** **ви** (your tickets), къщ**ата** **им** (their house). The full set of short possessives is now: **ми, ти, му, ѝ, ни, ви, им**. They are unstressed and always come straight after the noun. Neuter nouns take **-то**: куче**то** **им** (their dog).

reading.title: Къщата и колата
reading.text: Къщата им е голяма, а градината им е малка. — Билетите ви тук ли са? — Да, билетите ни са тук. Колата ни е стара, но е хубава.
reading.translation: Their house is big, and their garden is small. — Are your tickets here? — Yes, our tickets are here. Our car is old, but it is nice.

days.speakingPrompt: Talk about things shared by a group. Use ни, ви and им with the noun + article (колата ни, къщата им, билетите ви) and say whether each is голям, малък, нов or стар.

days.title: Saying мой and твой: the long possessives

grammar.title: Long possessives мой and твой
grammar.explanation: For emphasis or after **това е** (this is), Bulgarian uses **long possessives** that agree with the noun's **gender and number**. For 'my': **мой** (masc.), **моя** (fem.), **мое** (neut.), **мои** (plural). For 'your' (informal): **твой, твоя, твое, твои**. When the thing is definite, these take an article too: **моят** лаптоп, **моята** тетрадка, **моето** огледало, **моите** обувки. Compare the two systems: the short **лаптопът ми** and the long **моят лаптоп** both mean 'my laptop'; the long form is more emphatic. To ask 'whose?' use **чий** (masc.), **чия** (fem.), **чие** (neut.), **чии** (plural).

reading.title: Чий е лаптопът?
reading.text: — Чий е този лаптоп? — Това е моят лаптоп. — А тетрадката? — Тетрадката е твоя. Моята тетрадка е тук.
reading.translation: — Whose laptop is this? — This is my laptop. — And the notebook? — The notebook is yours. My notebook is here.

days.speakingPrompt: Pick up four objects and claim each with a long possessive: Това е моят/моята/моето/моите ... Then ask a friend 'Чий е...?' about one item and answer with твой/твоя/твое.

days.title: Putting it together: whose belongings are these?

vocab.exampleEn: My things are in the bag.
This is a gift for you.
The key is in the bag.

grammar.title: Combining имам, short and long possessives
grammar.explanation: Today you combine everything. Use **имам/нямам** to say what people have, then mark whose it is. Both systems work together: short possessives sit after the article (вещ**ите** **ми**, куфарче**то** **му**), while long possessives come before the noun for emphasis (**моите** вещи, **неговото** куфарче). With prepositions like **в** (in), **за** (for) and **с** (with), the noun keeps its plain or articled form — there are still **no cases**: подаръкът е **за** нея, ключът е **в** чантата. Add **също** (also) and **само** (only) to make richer sentences.

examples.russian: Имам само една чанта и вещите ми са в нея.
examples.english: I have only one bag, and my things are in it.
This is a gift for you.

reading.title: Подаръкът
reading.text: Имам подарък за теб. Вещите ми са в чантата, а подаръкът ти е тук. — Само за мен ли е? — Да, само за теб.
reading.translation: I have a gift for you. My things are in the bag, and your gift is here. — Is it only for me? — Yes, only for you.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe what is in your bag using имам and в, then say who owns each thing with a mix of short (ми, ти, му, ѝ) and long (мой, твой) possessives. Use също and само at least once.

days.title: Week 8 review: having and belonging

grammar.title: Recap: имам, short possessives and мой/твой
grammar.explanation: This week gave you the tools to talk about possession. **имам** (to have) and its negative **нямам** are regular: имам, имаш, има, имаме, имате, имат. The **short possessives** sit after the noun, which takes its article suffix: **ми, ти, му, ѝ, ни, ви, им** (чантата ми, куфарът му, колата ни, къщата им). They never change for the thing owned, only the owner. The **long possessives мой/твой** agree in gender and number (моят, моята, моето, моите) and are used for emphasis or after **това е**. Remember: Bulgarian has **no noun cases**, the article is a **suffix**, and the verb **съм** (е, са) is used freely: Чантата ми **е** нова.

examples.english: Do you have a key? — No, I don't.

reading.title: Чии са тези вещи?
reading.text: — Чия е тази чанта? — Чантата е моя. Вещите ми са в нея. — А този куфар? — Куфарът е негов, голям и тежък. — Имаш ли ключ? — Да, ключът ми е тук.
reading.translation: — Whose bag is this? — The bag is mine. My things are in it. — And this suitcase? — The suitcase is his, big and heavy. — Do you have a key? — Yes, my key is here.

days.speakingPrompt: Without notes, say three things you have and one you don't (имам/нямам), then claim four belongings using a mix of short possessives (ми, ти, му, ѝ, ни, ви, им) and long ones (мой, твой). Finish by asking a friend 'Чий е...?' about one item.
